coredata.dc:title: Sustainable fabrication of fluorescent carbon quantum dots as an optical amplifier in modern agriculture, anti-counterfeiting, food packing and intelligent pH detection

coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received a great deal of attention due to their distinctive luminescent features and exciting potential applications in the fields of agronomy, enhanced anti-counterfeiting, drug tracking and intelligent sensing and detection. The focus of the current effort is on easily transforming toxic, non-biodegradable waste expanded polystyrene foam (WEPS) into extremely luminescent CQDs and investigating their various applications. As a nano-priming agent for Cajanus cajan (L.), the green pigeon pea (GPP), WEPS derived CQDs are used. The outcomes showed that seed priming had beneficial effects at all CQDs concentrations (100–900 mg/L), including accelerated seed germination rate, increased shoot and root length, augmented root vigour, higher chlorophyll content and biomass accumulation in comparison to the control groups. The most crucial data coding can be protected under 365 nm UV light using the optimized CQDs concentration (700 mg/L) blue fluorescent ink. In addition, a straightforward sensing platform with high sensitivity and selectivity is needed for the detection of dual metal ions, as well as one with a "turn-on and turn-off" fluorescence (FL) response. Additionally, a composite film containing well-dispersed CQDs (700 mg/L) in polyvinyl alcohol and strong hydrogen-bond interaction is successfully developed to alleviate the FL quenching caused by CQD aggregation. The resulting film had extraordinary FL performance, good mechanical flexibility and high transparency (transmittance of 90 %). Even more remarkably, this luminescent film showed sensitive pH responsiveness, signifying that when pH is stimulated, the FL intensity changed. A smart pH-detector based on this luminescent film is logically created as a proof-of-concept for real-time sensing and detection of pH changes in human sweat. These results show that WEPS derived CQDs have enormous potential as nano-priming agents for seed germination, seedling development, anti-counterfeiting ink, food packaging and intelligent pH-detector in wearable, real-time health monitoring.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received a great deal of attention due to their distinctive luminescent features and exciting potential applications in the fields of agronomy, enhanced anti-counterfeiting, drug tracking and intelligent sensing and detection. The focus of the current effort is on easily transforming toxic, non-biodegradable waste expanded polystyrene foam (WEPS) into extremely luminescent CQDs and investigating their various applications. As a nano-priming agent for Cajanus cajan (L.), the green pigeon pea (GPP), WEPS derived CQDs are used. The outcomes showed that seed priming had beneficial effects at all CQDs concentrations (100–900 mg/L), including accelerated seed germination rate, increased shoot and root length, augmented root vigour, higher chlorophyll content and biomass accumulation in comparison to the control groups. The most crucial data coding can be protected under 365 nm UV light using the optimized CQDs concentration (700 mg/L) blue fluorescent ink. In addition, a straightforward sensing platform with high sensitivity and selectivity is needed for the detection of dual metal ions, as well as one with a "turn-on and turn-off" fluorescence (FL) response. Additionally, a composite film containing well-dispersed CQDs (700 mg/L) in polyvinyl alcohol and strong hydrogen-bond interaction is successfully developed to alleviate the FL quenching caused by CQD aggregation. The resulting film had extraordinary FL performance, good mechanical flexibility and high transparency (transmittance of 90 %). Even more remarkably, this luminescent film showed sensitive pH responsiveness, signifying that when pH is stimulated, the FL intensity changed. A smart pH-detector based on this luminescent film is logically created as a proof-of-concept for real-time sensing and detection of pH changes in human sweat. These results show that WEPS derived CQDs have enormous potential as nano-priming agents for seed germination, seedling development, anti-counterfeiting ink, food packaging and intelligent pH-detector in wearable, real-time health monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Plastic trash pollution is now a major issue on the global level and researchers are paying more attention to the issue. Numerous living things, including humans and aquatic, terrestrial and marine ecosystems are at risk from plastic garbage. Common plastic trash includes disposable surgical instruments, fast-food packaging and plastic bottles; these items are primarily made of polyethylene (PE), polyvinyl chloride (PVC), and other organic polymers [1–4]. However useful, many items end up as plastic waste, which is difficult to naturally breakdown and poses major pollution issues. Several million tons of plastics are produced more each year. One of the five all-purpose polymers, polystyrene produces hundreds of millions of kilograms a year. Waste expanded polystyrene foam (WEPS) is a thermoplastic hydrocarbon that is inert and widely used in thermal insulation and packaging. It can easily escape into the environment due to its low density [5–7].

WEPS burning is a convenient option, but it also helps to climate change by polluting the air and emitting carbon dioxide. In order to increase the added value of these waste products, a quick and easy process must be developed. We synthesized CQDs from WEPS and observed intense blue emission (PL). Nanotechnology is an emerging science with numerous uses in agriculture, energy, ecology and medicine. Due to their exceptional qualities, such as chemical stability, bio-compatibility, fabrication from a variety of precursors, low cost, low toxicity, small and uniform size, tunable functionalization, higher hydrophilicity, controllable photoluminescence and easy cell permeation CQDs are a promising new carbon-based material [8–10]. CQDs could be used for energy conversion, bioimaging, opto-electronics and catalysis. But there are still many significant challenges to overcome in the development of CQDs. Numerous nanomaterials (NMs) have been synthesized in recent years for use in agriculture, acting as sensors, insecticides, fertilizers and growth regulators to assure the high level of plant quality and productivity. The cutting-edge method known as seed nano-priming can enhance seed germination, seedling development and potentially raise stress resistance by utilizing NMs as priming agents before to sowing. Given that significant amounts of NMs are required as growth medium or foliar spray solution since they are applied during the entire plant life cycle, seed nano-priming may be an alternative method for sustainable agronomic practices. Because, in contrast to NM treatments that last a long time, nano-priming chemicals are only administered during the pre-sowing stage with little plant internalization. Theoretically, using a nano-priming method could reduce possible NM exposure in the environment, particularly NM exposure to people and animals who consume plants. Among a variety of NMs, metal and metal oxide nanoparticles (NPs) are the most often employed NMs for seed nano-priming. As an illustration, aged maize seeds' percentage of germination and the seedling vigour index are both increased by three times when green synthesized gold NPs are utilized as a nano-priming agent. As an illustration, aged maize seeds percentage of germination and the seedling vigour index are both increased by three times when green synthesized gold NPs are utilized as a nano-priming agent. It is still unusual to employ CQDs as a nano-priming agent and little study has been done on the mechanisms driving CQDs plant interactions with regard to different CQD types and plant species [11,12]. Recently, CQDs made from biomass and plastic waste have been synthesized and have successfully aided in the germination index and root elongation of seeds of barley, chickpeas, mung beans and wheat. Despite the fact that this work initially showed how CQDs had a good effect on seed germination through nano-priming, the underlying mechanism of CQD priming action is not completely understood and it is still unknown how this therapy can encourage further seedling development. Table 1 shows the literature survey for seed germination using various carbon dots [13–27].

Recently, a unique method of reducing FL quenching brought on by CQDs aggregation has been developed. In order to control their movement and provide a consistent distribution, CQDs are enclosed in polymer matrix [28]. Although encapsulating CQDs in polymer matrix could prevent aggregation-induced FL quenching and result in a comparatively uniform distribution in matrix, this would compromise some of the desirable fluorescent properties of CQDs, such as their sensitivity to pH stimulation [29]. The sensitivity of CQDs is significant for applications in wearable smart devices, environmental monitoring, medical diagnosis and life science among other fields [30,31]. As far as we know, investigations focusing on the pH responsive of the CQDs based polymer composites have been scarce. The solid-state CQDs based polymer composites blocked ionic interior, which rendered CQDs inactive to pH stimulation, is the enclosed system's inherent drawback. To fully utilize CQDs on polymer substrates, it is necessary to design materials based on CQDs that have great FL performance and sensitive pH-responsiveness.

For fabricating the desired CQDs-based materials in this study, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is optimally selected as the polymer substrate after taking into account its structure and characteristics. It is well known that the multiple hydroxyl groups present throughout PVA chains would provide sufficient sites for hydrogen bonding with CQDs, so restricting the CQDs movements and achieving the uniform dispersion in PVA matrix. Additionally, as the solution is being mixed, the hydroxyl-enriched chains would tie up a lot of water molecules and then naturally create an artificial channel for ion migration, guaranteeing the CQDs sensitivity to pH stimulation [32].

Information security, particularly encryption and anti-counterfeiting, has drawn increased attention as a result of its importance in safeguarding priceless records, goods, currencies and pharmaceuticals, along with the swift expansion of the contemporary economy and science [33–35]. Luminescence is used frequently for encryption and anti-counterfeiting because it is easy to develop, affordable and visible. As luminescent inks for security designs, many luminescent materials have been created and used. Their practical applicability is non-etheless limitations by their drawbacks, which include high toxicity, photothermal instability, high cost and limited yield. Recently, carbon dots (CQDs) have emerged as innovative luminous materials that stand out in the domains of data storage and optical encryption due to their numerous benefits, including high photostability, water solubility, low toxicity, low cost, strong simple production and good luminescence properties [36–38].

In order to tackle the aforementioned problems, WEPS-derived CQDs are first fabricated using a simple solvothermal process and in the meantime the desired CQDs (700 mg/L)@PVA film is made using a solution casting technique. As predicted, the well-dispersed CQDs appeared in the PVA matrix. Additionally, the composite film exhibits outstanding mechanical flexibility, excellent luminous qualities and sensitive pH responsiveness in the given pH range (4–8), demonstrating its promising potential in intelligent sensing as well as detection, food packaging and anti-counterfeiting. Table 2 depicts the literature survey on CQDs incorporated in different types of composite films for food packaging applications [39–47].

Waste expanded polystyrene foam (WEPS), HNO3, dichloromethane (DCE), PVA and HgCl2. Except WEPS, the polymer, PVA (MW = 1500 g/mol) is brought from Sigma-Aldrich, India (Mw = 85,000–124,000 g/mol, 99 % hydrolyzed). Analytical grade chemicals and reagents are used without further purification. Large amounts of WEPS are collected from the nearby Tumkur University campus, Tumkur, India. Green pigeon pea (GPP) seeds are purchased from local market in Tumkur.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of luminescent CQDs from waste expanded polystyrene
ce:section.ce:para: The solvothermal approach is used to fabricate CQDs. Often 0.1 gm of WEPS are dissolved in 10 mL of DCE to produce a clear solution. The resulting solution is then mixed with 20 mL of HNO3. The resultant mixture is then transferred to a 25 mL autoclave lined with poly tetrafluoroethylene (Teflon) and heated at 200 °C for 4 h. The solution is remained transparent after the reactor have automatically cooled to room temperature (RT) and in daylight it appears pale yellow. In order to produce CQDs, the resulting solution is boiled to eliminate DCE. Fig. 1 a shows a schematic illustration of the fabrication of CQDs.

Using PVA and CQDs, the anti-counterfeiting ink is fabricated. The dispersive substance is essential for two reasons: viscosity and dispersibility. Dispersibility plays a vital role of ensuring transparency by dispersing the phosphors in solution, while viscosity contributes to the sticky nature of the paper used for printing. PVA is gently dissolved in 30 mL of deionized (DI) water while being vigorously stirred utilizing a magnetic stirrer until a clear solution is obtained. Next, CQDs are dissolved in the solution. The transparent, clear security ink is then obtained.

ce:list-item.ce:label: a.
ce:list-item.ce:para: Simple brush mode: A illustration of handwriting and the screen-printing technique is shown in Fig. 1b. The uniform mixture is employed as fluorescent ink for subsequent writing, creating drawings and printing. Some written content and pictures are drawn using a writing brush dipped in CQDs solution on papers having no fluorescent backgrounds. The Canon EOS 1500D Digital SLR Camera (EF S18-55 Lens) is then used to photograph the paper under daylight and 365 nm UV light.

ce:list-item.ce:label: b.
ce:list-item.ce:para: Screen-print mode: CQDs security ink is dropped at one end of the printing screen plate and a squeezer is used to apply pressure on the ink part on the plate. The ink is transferred from the mesh of the patterned section to the substrate by the squeezer during the movement. The ink can print on a variety of patterns on a diverse surfaces made of different materials. After curing, the patterns emit a consistent, intense blue light at a wavelength of 365 nm, but they are transparent and invisible in daylight, exhibiting strong concealing characteristics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PVA@CQDs films for food packing applications
ce:section.ce:para: PVA@CQDs films are moulded in the manner shown in Fig. 1c. PVA granules (10 g) are first well mixed with 40 mL of DI. To dissolve PVA, the mixture is then heated to 90 °C for 1 h. After that, 40 mL of PVA aqueous solution is combined with 5 mL of CQDs aqueous solution (700 mg/L), which is then agitated for 60 min and sonicated for 30 min. Finally, the mixed solution is poured into a Petri dish and dry for 24 h at 50 °C. After drying, the PVA@CQDs composite films are peeled off the Petri dish and stored in zip wrappers for the subsequent studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth of seedlings and their germination studies
ce:section.ce:para: In order to begin, all of the GPP seeds are sterilized for 5 min in mercuric chloride (HgCl2) solution to get rid of any bacterial contamination. The excess HgCl2 residue from the seeds has been removed by washing them in DI water for 15–20 min. The ability of GPP seeds to germinate has been studied using a series of measurements. Dry GPP seeds are first washed with HgCl2 and water before being put in direct contact with various CQDs concentration solutions (100, 300, 500, 700 and 900 mg/L) (Fig. 1d). Our exploratory experiment served as the basis for the concentration selection. To keep measurements equivalent, 20 seeds in an equal number are germinated on several petri dishes containing 5 mL of an aqueous CQDs solution. As a control, the seeds are soaked in DI water. To assess the systems repeatability, all experiments are carried out three times. A thread is used to measure the length of the emerging roots. For the analysis, measurements of several pairs of seed roots and shoots that sprouted from the germination of seeds between days 1 and 11 are used.

20 GPP sprouts are chosen at random and their length and diameter are measured using various CQDs doses of 100, 300, 500, 700 and 900 mg/L. A ruler is used to measure the length of GPP sprouts and an vernier calliper is used to measure the diameter of bean sprouts during the measurement span of 1–11 days. 20 GPP sprouts are weighed after 11 days and then kept in a dry box at a constant temperature (60 °C) for 2 days before being removed and weighed once more.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of chlorophyll content in leaves of GPP seeds by using acetone
ce:section.ce:para:
As stated earlier (Arnon, 1949 [48]), 10 mL of 80 % acetone is used to grind 0.1 g of freshly chopped leaves. It is then centrifuged for 5 min at 5000-10,000 rpm. Once the residue became colourless, the supernatant is transferred and the process is repeated. In comparison to the solvent (acetone), absorbance of the solution was red at 480, 645 and 663 nm. The following equation [49] is used to determine the concentrations of chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b and total chlorophyll:
Chlorophyll a: 12.7(A663) – 2.69(A645)
Chlorophyll b: 22.9(A645) – 4.68(A663)
(1) Total Chlorophyll: 20.2(A645) + 8.02(A663)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of different parameters using seed germination assay
ce:section.ce:para:
The following formulae have been used to determine different parameters for GPP seed, including germination ability (GA), germination index (GI), percentage inhabitation growth (PIG), seed Vigour index I (VI–I) and seed Vigour II (SV-II) [50].

VI-I = Germination % × Length of seedling (cm) (5).

SV II = Germination % × Dry weight of seedling (mg) (6).where St and Sc represent the total number of seeds that have germinated in the CQDs and control samples, respectively and Lt and Lc represent the length of seeds that have germinated in the CQDs and control samples, respectively. The terms Cc and Cd correspond to the average root length of seeds found in the control and CQDs samples, respectively. All measurements are made when the root and coleoptile lengths, reached their maximums taken after 11 days [51].

After each measurement is conducted in triplicate, the findings are displayed as the mean standard deviation. Utilizing one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and the Tukey test (P < 0.05), the experimental data are statistically analyzed.

The QY of the CQDs are determined using a previously known methodology [52,53]. To minimize the effects of re-absorption, the CQDs and reference quinine sulphate (QS) are prepared in 5 various concentrations. When observed at the excitation wavelength of 321 nm, each of these concentrations displayed an absorbance value > 0.10. QS is dissolved in 0.1 M of H2SO4 (QY: 54 %, index of refraction (ɳ): 1.33) to produce the reference solution. To determine the QY of the CQDs, the integrated FL intensities recorded at the same excitation wavelength and the samples absorbance value are compared to the references. The equation below is used to estimate the QY of the synthesized CQDs:

where Φ: QY, A: for the absorbance, I: the integrated FL emission intensity, m: slope and η:refractive index of the solvent medium, the subscript CQD and R: sample and references respectively.

The morphology and size of the CQDs are examined using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) (JEOL JEM 2100 system operating at a voltage of 200 kV). A Nano-ZS90 Zetasizer (Malvern, UK) is used to observe the size distribution of CQDs. A step scan of 0.02°/min, ranging from 10 to 80° steps, is used to analyze the powder X-ray diffraction (Shimadzu 7000 powder X-ray diffractometer (Cuka radiation; l = 1.5406 Å) pattern. Raman spectroscopy is carried out on a confocal RAMAN spectrometer model 500 (STR 500), using a 532 nm excitation laser wavelength. The elemental composition of CQDs is carried out by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, by using a modified AlK source and an XR4 dual anode in a vacuum generator (VG) ESCALABMKII spectrometer. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra are analyzed using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan) supplied by Shimadzu Corporation. The FL spectra are acquired by Horiba spectrofluorimeter, a 450 W xenon lamp instrument. Using transient steady state spectroscopy (FLS1000, Edinburgh Instruments, UK) together with a photometric integrating sphere-assisted method, the FL QY of CQDs and CQDs@PVA film is determined.

The XRD pattern of CQDs shows two major prominent peaks situated at ∼14.99° and ∼22.21° (Fig. 2 a). According to Bragg's Law, the broad peak at 23.3 has an inter-layer spacing of 0.38 nm, which is larger than that of graphitic carbon (d(002) = 0.36 nm) (JCPDS file No. 75–1621). The minor peak at 14.99° is moved towards the peak that corresponded to the (002) plane in graphitic carbon by the insertion of oxygen-containing groups and water molecules to the carbon layers [54]. The amorphous nature of CQDs and the existence of functional groups containing oxygen are generally demonstrated by the broad peak shape and peak position with the XRD pattern.

Raman spectroscopy is an intriguing equipment for determining crystallinity or amorphicity of material and consequently the degree of disorder. Raman spectrum of CQDs shows two peaks at 1362 and 1605 cm−1, which attributed to the D and G bands, respectively (Fig. 2b). Vibrations of carbon atoms with pendent bonds can be seen in a terminating plane of disordered graphite or glassy carbon. The two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal lattice of sp2-bonded carbon atoms is vibrating and this phenomenon is referred as G band in terms of graphite. The ID/IG band intensity ratio is employed to quantify sp3 hybridized carbon atoms as well as to compare defect and disorder density in CQDs [55,56]. The integrated area of D peak to that of G peak is determined by ID/IG ratio and is found to be to 2.12. This suggests that the EPS underwent a significant level of amination, chlorination and oxygenation problems during the solvothermal carbonization process. Despite the fact that the values of the ID/IG ratio are not clearly related to the concentrations of nitrogen, chlorine and oxygen, the results show that disorder has a significant impact on the ID/IG ratio.

The microscopic properties of the carbon dots were determined using TEM analysis and are shown in Fig. 2c. The results indicate that the particles are spherical, homogeneously monodispersed with particle size in the range ∼5–7 nm. The HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 2c) showed distinct lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of ∼0.22 nm corresponding to the (002) plane characteristic of graphene.

FTIR spectroscopy and XPS are used to examine the structure, elemental and surface composition of the CQDs. The spectra of CQDs exhibits the stretching vibrations of –OH and –NH2 at 3415 cm−1 and 3186 cm−1, respectively. The distinctive peaks at 2988 and 2925−1, respectively, indicate the symmetric and asymmetric methylene (–CH2–) stretching vibrations [57]. The stretching (amide bonds C=O), bending vibrations (N–H), stretching vibrations (C=N), bending modes (C–N) and symmetric stretching vibrations (C–O–C), respectively, are represented by the absorption peaks at 1672, 1637, 1559, 1396 and 1046−1 (Fig. 2d) [58–62]. Thus, hydrophilic groups like –OH, –COOH and –NH2 are present in the CQDs.

Findings of FTIR are additionally validated by XPS. As shown in Fig. 2e, the XPS survey spectrum reveals that the CQDs are composition of 65 % carbon, 10 % oxygen and 25 % nitrogen. The C 1s spectra of the CQDs are deconvoluted into 4 peaks at energies of ∼285.12 eV (C–C/C=C), ∼286.86 eV (C–O/C–N), ∼288.34 eV (CONH) and ∼289.27 eV (C=O/C=N), as shown in Fig. 2f. As shown in Fig. 2g, the CQDs XPS N 1s spectra has two peaks at 398.24 and 401.08 eV that are attributed to C–N=C (pyridinic N) and N–H, respectively. In earlier investigations, it has been shown that C–N/C=N groups improve RTP [63]. In accordance with Fig. 2h, the CQDs XPS O 1s spectra have two peaks that corresponds to C=O (531.74 eV) and C–O (533.21 eV) bonds.

By using UV–vis absorption and FL spectroscopy, the optical characteristics of the CQDs are examined; the related to spectra are displayed in Fig. 3 a. Figure demonstrates that the UV component of the UV–vis spectrum is dominated by a broad optical absorption peak. The linked carbonyl groups (C=C/C=O) in CQDs possibly undergone π-π* and n-π* electron transitions as a result of this peak. The CQDs show the best excitation and emission wavelengths for the PL spectra, located at 361 and 422 nm, respectively. The CQDs solutions are shown in the inset of Fig. 3a under 365 nm UV light (on the right) and daylight (on the left). When exposed to daylight, the CQDs solution is noticeably clear, transparent and bright pale yellow. It also exhibits a strong blue luminescence under UV 365 nm light, further demonstrating their blue FL properties. Fig. 3b displays the emission spectra of CQDs excited under different excitation wavelengths between 290 and 440 nm. The CQDs exhibit a normal excitation dependent PL behaviour with the greatest emission peak when stimulated by 365 nm UV light. When the excitation wavelength rises from 300 to 400 nm, the emission peak moves to longer wavelengths between 416 and 474 nm, which is consistent with the majority of fluorescent CQDs previously described [64]. This phenomenon might be connected to the anti-stokes PL and the complexity of the several surface states in CQDs [65,66].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion sensing aptitude of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The clearly defined emission characteristics of CQDs encouraged us to explore their potential use in the field of sensing, particularly for selective metal ion sensing. Metal ions presence has established an imperative character in a specific level of the physiologic functioning of existing organisms. Different diseases, including typhoid, dysentery, worm disease, guinea worm, cholera and diarrhoea, have emerged in living things as a result of exceeding the specific concentration. Our drinking water is the primary source of intake for different metal ions. As a result, it is crucial to find a quick and accurate way to detect even minute amounts of such dangerous metal ions. In this study, the emission characteristics of CQDs are used for the selective sensing of metal ions. Under RT condition, the FL emission behaviour of CQDs is examined for the purpose of testing in the presence of a 5 mM solution containing the ions K+, Na+, Ni+, Mg2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Cd2+, NH4+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Fe2+ and Co2+. The DI water has been selected as a medium for various nitrate salts containing various metal ions. With 0.5 g of CQDs in 100 mL of water, the pH of the reaction media is kept at 8. When compared to CQDs without any metal ions, the inclusion of Cd2+ showed an improvement in the FL emission intensity [67]. However, the FL emission intensity of CQD has been significantly quenched by the presence of the Cu2+ ion. The emission intensities of the other metal ions have not significantly changed. The results showed that our fabricated CQDs have turn-on sensors for Cd2+ and turn-off sensors for Cu2+ ions (Fig. 3c).

The physical changes in the CQDs brought on by the presence of Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions have significantly contributed to an enhanced and quenching of FL emission intensity. Because Cu2+ ions have a great affinity for the hydroxyl, carboxyl and amine groups on the surface of CQDs, this association has resulted in to quenching in the emission of FL intensity of CQDs in the presence of Cu2+ ions. The tendency for CQD and copper ions to form chelate complexes in a steady state has further stimulated differences in the surroundings of CQDs and grouped the elementary charges in between the bound atoms. This potent interaction between CQDs and Cu2+ ions has elevated non-radiative charge recombination in CQDs and resulted in intensity quenching. Another potential cause of quenching is the physical interaction between the Cu2+ metal ion and the CQD, which has been shown to hinder plasmon coupling and electric field distribution in the intensity of the FL (Fig. 4 a). The intensity behaviour has also been evaluated in the presence of CQDs as a function of Cu2+ ion concentration. It can be seen from the graph that the peak intensity of the CQDs' luminescence increased as Cu2+ concentration is decreased from 0.001 μM to 5 nM (Fig. 3d). On varying the concentration from 0.001 μM to 1 μM, the peak splitting is not observed. This might be brought on by the entire process of molecule absorption and CQD stabilization. The emission peaks present in the luminescence spectra of CQDs have disappeared due to the total absorption of molecules. The excitation peak in the spectrum is clearly seen when the concentration of Cu2+ ions is further reduced from 0.001 μM to 5 nM. The emission intensity has increased as the amount of metal ions in the reaction system has decreased. It is clear from this that 0.01 mM is the concentration required for the full absorption and stabilization of CQDs. CQDs are only partially absorbed as a result of further changes to the Cu2+ ion concentration.

In the instance of Cd2+ ions, the process of increasing emission intensity has suggested that CQDs have been introduced with intrinsic radiative combination decay rate. This behavioural shift is mostly the result of CQDs higher rate of excitation when Cd2+ ions are present. The possibility of a chemical reaction between Cd2+ ions with the localized surface plasmon resonance on the surface of CQDs has also contributed to the amplification of the CQDs emission peak. This FL spectroscopy enhancement pattern that occurs in the presence of CQDs is a very intriguing, novel and instructive phenomena (Fig. 4a). Further testing of the intensity behaviour in the presence of CQDs as a function of Cd2+ ion concentration is shown in Fig. 3e. Cd2+ ions in lower concentrations, between 1 μM and 0.001 μM, have bifurcated peaks with similar emission intensities. These peaks indicate that the energy may have been released in two stages. This is mostly caused by the system having extra energy from the complexation of Cd2+ ions with CQDs. By utilizing normalized emission intensity, the FL enhancement factor and FL quenching factor for Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions are determined and the results are 1.5 and 0.990, respectively (Fig. 3f). It could be concluded that Cd2+ ions coordinate electrostatically and interact with CQDs, allowing CQD Cd2+ aggregation to form conjugated amorphous masses that produce the aggregation-induced high FL emission (Fig. 4b). These results are even more noteworthy because they demonstrate that the CQD as it is synthesized had extraordinary selectivity for Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions in addition to dual FL response features.

The effectiveness of the CQDs system in the presence of Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions is additionally investigated, as well as the interference effect of other metal ions. 5 mM solutions of various metal ions have been made using DI water for the measurement. The amount of CQDs emission in the prepared metal ion solution has been measured with the presence of Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions. According to the observed photoluminescence spectrum (Fig. S1), the effectiveness of Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions is unaffected by the presence of other metal ions. By conducting the same experiment three times, the reliability of the constructed system has also been verified. The following mathematical expression has been used to plot the Sterne-Volmer calibration curve between F/Fo and the concentration of a specific metal ion (Figs. S2a and b):
(9)

F
= 1 +
k
S V
τ
[ Q ]
where t is the life of the excited state, Q is the concentration, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant, F0 is the emission intensity of CQDs without any metal ions, F is the emission intensity of CQDs with metal ions and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant. By using the 3s IUPAC standard method, it is determined that the detection and quantitative limits of the developed sensor are 0.16, 0.63 nM for cadmium ions and 0.25, 0.92 nM for copper ions.

In the presence of Cu2+ and Cd2+, the FL switching behaviour of CQDs can be used to create numerous molecular logic gates that carry out Boolean algebraic logic operations [68,69]. When Cu2+ is used as the single input signal, the straightforward single input molecular logic gate NOT can be built (Fig. 5 a). By using Cu2+ and Cd2+ as inputs 1 and 2, respectively, it is possible to realize the multiple input logic gate IMPLICATION. Cu2+ and Cd2+ are taken as 1, while their absence is taken as 0. As a result, the output is calculated using the highest FL as 1 and the matching quenched FL as 0. As seen in Fig. 5b, considerable FL quenching can only be observed when Cu2+ is present and Cd2+ is absent, or input (1, 0), whereas in the case of other inputs (0/0, 0/1, 1/1), the output remain 1. Fig. 5c depicts the output FL intensity at 430 nm, the truth table and the logic symbol.

In addition to their extraordinary FL performance, CQDs pH responsiveness is essential to their potential use in intelligent sensing and detection. As a result, the correlative behaviours are assessed following stimulation at various pH levels (pH = 5–12). As seen in Fig. 6 a and b, the CQD solution is completely transparent to visible light. As the pH varied from 3 to 12, the FL intensity which emits UV light constantly dropped. The intensity is accordingly maximum in an acidic environment with a pH of 5 and lowest in an alkaline environment with a pH of 12. At a maximum excitation wavenumber of 410 nm, the detail FL intensities are found for a quantitative analysis. As anticipated, the luminous intensity demonstrated an exceptional responsiveness to pH stimulation (Fig. 6c) and a linear decrease in FL intensity is observed as pH increased (Fig. 6d and e). The main factor causing that shift may be the different levels of de-protonation brought on by pH variations, which directly affect the levels of ionization of functional groups in the CQDs framework and effect the variation of FL properties [70]. What happens if the pH is repeatedly stimulated while the FL intensity is maintained? This pH responsive CQDs can make meaning out of it. In accordance with the plan, the repeated-regulation processing permitted the detection of CQD fluorescent intensities (Fig. 6f). Notably, the intensity consistently remained in a stable zone over several circulations while the pH value remained constant. With the help of this confirmation, it can be certain that the CQDs not only displayed sensitive pH responsiveness, but also consistently maintained their luminous property after repeated pH stimulation regulation. This excellent performance increases CQDs' potential for reliable pH fluctuation detection [71]. Does the luminous intensity still remain stable after repeated pH stimulation regulation? In terms of these pH responsive CQDs, it has significance. The repeated-regulation processing successfully detected the FL intensities of CQDs (Fig. 6f). Notably, the intensity consistently remained in a steady zone over many circulations while the pH value remained constant. With the help of this confirmation, it is certain that the CQDs not only displayed sensitive pH responsiveness, but also consistently maintained their luminous property after repeated pH stimulation regulation. This excellent performance broadens the possibilities for CQDs in the precise detection of pH variation [71].

At the 321 nm excitation wavelength, the absorbance as well as integrated intensity values for CQDs and references (QS) are plotted and the resulting slopes are computed as shown in Fig. S3. High linearity can be seen in the measured data, which also has an intercept value close to zero. Using the data, the QY of the CQDs is found to be 28.65 %.

The long term stability experiments are conducted to assess the photobleaching resistance of the CDs by subjecting them to continuous UV irradiation (36 mW/cm2) for 0–90 min. The obtained results clearly indicated that, the fluorescent intensity of the CQDs showed a minimal change even after 90 min of UV exposure (Fig. S5a). The normalized intensity and digital images of the CQDs solution are presented in Figs. S5b and c, respectively. Fig. S5d demonstrate the evaluation of the photostability of CDs by monitoring their fluorescence intensity across different aging time intervals. Remarkably, there are no noticeable alterations in the fluorescence intensity of CQDs throughout the aging period spanning from day 1 to day 40. The persistent absence of photo-bleaching, even after 40 days, suggests that the structure of CQDs exhibits remarkable resistance and is not readily affected by environmental factors. The normalized intensity and digital images of aged aqueous solutions of CQDs under 365 nm UV light are depicted Figs. S5e and f. The overall results clearly indicated that, CQDs are robust resistance to photobleaching and suitability for long-term storage and utilization.

New possibilities for useful AC applications are made possible by the exceptional PL property of synthesized CQDs. Over time, there has been a significant increase in the counterfeiting and fabrication of important goods and documents, including diplomas, money, well-known brands, medicines, cuisines and so forth. This has had a negative impact on humanity, the global economy and social development on a large scale. In order to solve these limitations, a number of fluorescent-based materials have been used in AC applications; however, problems with toxicity, spectral overlap, low quantum efficiency and luminescence quenching still exist [72–74]. We made luminescent-based security inks to show the viability of the synthesized CQDs for AC applications. Fig. 7 a shows the various concentrations of CQDs luminous ink. The creation of anti-counterfeiting patterns on paper is created, using different concentrations of luminous ink. Fig. 7b displays the AC patterns developed using various CQD security ink concentrations. Of all the photos, the AC pattern created using CQDs security ink exhibits the most strong FL emission. which, when exposed to UV light, emits a bright blue emission visible to the human eye. Additional AC designs are created using a straightforward brush mode process on a variety of materials, including stainless steel, wine glass, ceramic, filter paper, compact disc and glass beaker and they are then seen in natural light and UV 365 nm light (Fig. 7c). Under UV 365 nm light, however, the created patterns may be seen as distinct, exact, sharp luminescence patterns that are invisible to the naked eye in ambient light. Investigations into the stability of the AC pattern are also conducted. Fig. 7d shows that the AC patterns on the glass bottle are preserved across various time periods (day 1 to day 180). The pattern may be seen at UV 365 nm. The homogeneous distribution and emission strength of the AC pattern on the glass bottle, however, could still be seen after 180 days of ageing at RT (Fig. 7d). These results show how synthetic CQD ink may be useful for encoding sensitive data with covert security features.

Fig. 8 a shows the AC labels made using a simple brush mode on a piece of paper that has been subjected to strong UV rays for 10–120 min. The findings show that the FL intensity is steady for up to 60 min before it starts to decline. To assess the chemical stability of the ink, patterns created on OHP are subjected to CH3OH, C2H5OH, C6H5CH3, H2O and C3H6O treatment [75]. We found that the pattern is quite resilient against all of the chemicals used for testing, despite having the ability to damage the polymeric substance employed as the base for printing the design. Fig. 8b displays digital photographs of the pattern taken after chemical treatment. We found that the design holds up well against all of the testing substances. to show that the synthesized CQDs are useful for AC applications. The anti-counterfeiting patterns are created on paper using luminous ink that has been pH-treated at 1, 3, 6, 10 and 14 (Fig. 8c). As observed in the picture, the AC pattern which is created using pH 6 treated ink displays the highest intensity FL emission and can release a bright blue emission visible to the unaided eye when exposed to UV light. Additionally, ink that has been treated with pH (3 and 10) exhibits less intense FL emission than ink that has been treated with pH (1 and 14), which exhibits no FL emission.

To demonstrate the method for screen print anti-counterfeiting application patterns on a variety of substrates, such as cellulose paper, painted wood and binding covers, using luminous security ink. Fig. 9 a shows digital images of a pattern on diverse surfaces exposed under daylight and 365 nm UV radiation. As seen in the images, the pattern created on these surfaces emits vivid blue. Due to reflection from the substrate and the interaction of luminous security ink with substrate, the colour appearance also considerably varies depending on the type of substrate [76]. Fig. 9b shows that the AC patterns on the glass bottle are preserved across various time intervals (days 1 through 100). The pattern may be visualized at UV 365 nm. The homogeneous distribution and emission strength of the AC pattern on the glass bottle, however, could still be seen after 180 days of ageing at RT (Fig. 9b). These results show how synthetic CQD ink may be useful for encoding sensitive data with concealed security characteristics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Seed germination activity in presence of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A study of the growth of GPP seeds in five different CQD concentrations, ranging from 100 to 900 mg/L, is conducted to visualize this effect (Fig. 9a). Fig. 10 a provides a reference image of seedling development following various seed priming treatments (100–900 mg/L) at various time intervals (1–11 days), where a clear phenotypic difference can be seen between seedlings produced from seeds primed with control sample and CQDs solution. As evidence of the low phytotoxicity of the WEPS CQDs in this study, established seedlings still outperform the control group in terms of the aforementioned phenotypes when exposed to high concentrations of CQDs (700 mg/L) during seed priming. However, prepared CQDs can interfere with plant development, particularly with the metabolic process, as previous research have also found.The germination rate is significantly impacted by concentration, particle size and physicochemical characteristics [77]. The shape, size and surface functionalization of the selected CQDs have all been used to explain their nature.The surface properties and surface area of particular CQDs have also been found to be significantly correlated with the phytotoxic activity of germination seeds. Further promoting the formation of plant nuclei is a carboxyl group that covers the surface of CQDs. In addition to CQDs, the nature of the seed has been a major factor in the capacity of seeds to germinate. For instance, mature dried seeds have a higher capacity to absorb water, which helps to accelerate cellular metabolism and growth. Additionally, the sort of micro-nuclei found in the chosen seeds affects the way rootlets emerge from seeds.

Further research by Refs. [78,79] found that large doses of CQDs did not significantly promote plant growth. The physiological processes of GPP seed are disrupted by the higher quantities of CQDs, which may have contributed to the increased formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and oxidative stress [80]. High CQDs concentrations may also likely to aggregate on seed coats, obstructing water absorption and thus slowing seed germination and seedling growth [81]. From the other hand, the growth indices of pea seedlings improved as well when the dosage of CQDs is increased from 100 to 500 mg/mL. At low concentrations, it is thought that when CQD concentrations increase, more CQDs will interact with GPP seeds, giving them an overall beneficial benefit. Based on the results so far, it is believed that 700 mg/L of CQD is the optimum dosage for improving seed germination and seedling growth. In order to emphasize the effects of CQDs priming even more, future analyses of the biochemical indices of GPP seeds and seedlings will largely focus on the group treated with the optimum CQDs concentration as well as the control group. Numerous measurements have been made in order to better understand how CQDs influence GA, including average root length, shoot length, GP, GI, PIG, VI-I and SV-II (Table 3 ).

An increase in seed inhibition and germination rate is expected to be favourable in terms of seedling growth and biomass accumulation. The length, diameter and biomass of GPP seedlings are measured after harvest and represented in Fig. 11 to confirm the aforementioned hypothesis. Overall, the results demonstrated that CQDs have an effect on seedling growth that is dose-dependent and groups treated with CQDs (100–900 mg/L) outperformed the control group. As seen in Fig. 11a, seedling shoot and root lengths grew until the CQDs concentration for seed priming reached 700 mg/L and then significantly decreased when the concentrations rose to 900 mg/L. When compared to the control group, the group that is given CQDs at a dosage of 700 mg/L significantly increased both the shoot (P < 0.001) and the root (P < 0.001) lengths. In comparison to the control group, the group treated with CQDs at 700 and 900 mg/L increased their shoot diameter by 12% and 9%, respectively (Fig. 11b). All of the CQDs treated groups had considerably bigger root diameters than the control group, indicating that CQDs priming might promote the development of a stronger root system, which is crucial for promoting seedling growth. Fig. 11c displays the shoot and root's fresh biomass. The group treated with CQDs at a concentration of 700 mg/L shows the most substantial accumulation of biomass, with values that are 50 % and 39 % greater than the control, respectively. Similar to this, when seeds primed with 700 mg/L CQDs solution produced shoot and root, the dry biomass risen by 60 % and 56 %, respectively, in comparison to the control (Fig. 11d). It can be concluded that CQDs priming can hasten the accumulation of seedling biomass, a crucial indicator throughout plant growth.

In the presence of CQDs, the observational parameters, such as germination percentage, germination index and nucleus growth inhibition, have been examined as a function of concentration. According to the findings, each seed in a particular range of concentration had a germination percentage that is almost 100 %. Additionally, the rapid growth of nuclei in GPP seeds in the presence of CQDs has been further substantiated by the large increase in the values of the germination index and root length. Average root length is found to be equivalent in all cases, however growth is at its highest at a concentration of 700 mg/L. In comparison to the control system, seed germination is sped up in all CQD concentrations. Due to the large increase in the water absorption capacity of GPP seeds, the value of the germination index is determined to be at its highest in the case of 700 ppm, or 103 %. In virtually all instances, the value of percentage inhibition growth (PIG) is determined to be insignificant. In some concentrations, the improvement in biocompatibility is found to be greater than 100 % compared to the control standard. The graph indicates that the synthetic CQDs are very biocompatible by nature and have demonstrated excellent consistency during numerous experiments. Since CQDs are non-toxic, agricultural fields have preferred to employ them to strengthen rootlets (Fig. 11e and f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observation on the migration of CQDs in bean sprouts
ce:section.ce:para: We noticed a CQD migration behaviour in the GPP sprouts during the cultivation process. Following 5 days of cultivation in a 700 mg/L CQDs solution, the roots of the GPP sprouts began to generate a clear blue glow when exposed to UV light. The CQDs are evenly distributed throughout the root after being absorbed for 11 days by the GPP sprouts. Over time, the CQDs have migrated to the stems of the bean sprouts and finally reached the leaves [82]. The FL intensity of the carbon dots minimizes noticeably as the position of the GPP sprouts rises. As we watched the bean sprouts absorb carbon dots, we noticed that there is a dazzling glow between the stem and root of the plant. CQDs are first enriched at the end of the root and then start to migrate to the leaf portion during segment migration (Fig. 12 a–c). They are then enriched at the end of the stem and migrate to the leaf part.

In the process of transforming light energy into chemical energy, the green pigment chlorophyll plays a significant role. The effectiveness of light absorption and the production of carbohydrates during photosynthesis are both improved by a plant's increased chlorophyll concentration Table 4 , which also makes future plant growth more favourable [83]. As seen in Fig. 13 , plant treated with CQDs have higher chlorophyll content than control plants, with a maximum chlorophyll content of 700 mg/L after 11 days of incubation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transparency test using PVA@CQDs composite films
ce:section.ce:para: From a practical perspective, transparency of any UV-blocking materials or films is now essential. With these considerations in mind, we embedded WEPS CQDs into bio-compatible and bio-degradable polymer (PVA) to produce highly transparent, flexible and thin PVA@CQDs composite films. Fig. 14 a & b exhibit photographs demonstrating the transparency of PVA film and PVA@CQDs CFs at increasing CQD concentrations (100–900 mg/L). The photographic photos demonstrate this, since the translucent nature of the PVA@CQDs CFs is not significantly affected by increasing CQD concentrations up to 900 mg/L, even though fabricated composite films are very transparent to visible light. To comprehend the similar FL characteristics of a liquid PVA@CQDs mixture, we performed a photophysical evaluation of thin films made of solid PVA@CQDs composites with varying CQD fluorescent constituent concentrations (100–900 mg/L). The FL emission profile of PVA film and PVA@CQDs composite films at different CQDs concentrations is shown in Fig. 14c. It is noted that the pure PVA film does not show a recognizable FL emission band in the visible spectrum when excited at 365 nm. Even with a small (900 mg/L) integration of fluorescent CQDs into a PVA film, a broad emission band about 422 nm appears in PVA@CQDs composite films. This emission band gets more powerful at higher CQD concentrations in PVA@CQDs composite films between 100 and 900 mg/L. The most intense FL band seen in PVA@CQDs composite films (900 mg/L) indicates that it may be able to transfer absorbed UV energy into the visible region by adding CQDs into PVA films.

Various quantities of CQDs are embedded to the PVA to test ability to block UV light and CFs are produced in accordance with the procedure described in the experimental section. The UV–vis analysis is carried out in transmittance (%) mode to ascertain the UV blocking ability of PVA and various concentrations CQDs CFs. Fig. 14d depicts the transmittance plots of pure PVA and PVA@CQDs CFs. with increasing solid-state CQDs concentrations (100–900 mg/L). It is found that the UV region (250–650 nm) of pure PVA film exhibits greatest UV light transmission (T = 82 %). However, pristine PVA film had some UV-C (230–278 nm) absorption capacity. The PVA@CQDs (900 mg/L) film is able to drastically change the UV absorption capacity and reduce the transmission of light in the UV-C (230–278 nm), UV-B (278–320 nm) and portions of the UVA (315–650 nm) region. On the contrary, hand, the insertion of CQDs into PVA film at even the smallest amounts caused a severe shift. Additionally, by incorporating progressively more CQDs into PVA films, the UV blocking ability of PVA@CQDs CFs is enhanced. While blocking 100 % of UV-C (230–278 nm) and UV-B (278–320 nm) light, the film PVA@CQDs (700 mg/L) efficiently absorbed 20–60 % of UV light in the UV-A (315–400 nm) region. Finally, the saturation point for the PVA@CQDs (900 mg/L) composite film is achieved at 900 mg/L increased concentration of CQDs in the PVA film. There are very minor fluctuations in the absorption values throughout the UV area. Also demonstrated in Fig. 14e are the various deformation-forms (flexible performance). Impressively, the surface of the PVA@CQDs film showed nearly no structural or FL fault under any bending, rolling, twisting, or folding. These behaviour showed that the final film has outstanding mechanical characteristics as well as stable luminous behaviour even under various deformation-forms, which is essential for its use in wearable fluorescent devices [84,85].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thickness measurement study of PVA and PVA@CQDs composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Thickness of the fabricated films is important when considering the uses of UV blocking films in various fields. In addition, thickness and film transparency are correlated. The flexibility of films will improve with decreasing film thickness and will be easier to mould into any form or size. Thickness measurements are done for PVA and PVA@CQDs (700 mg/L) composite films in order to understand the impact of increasing concentration of added UV blocking agent on the thickness of pure PVA films and results are summarized in Table 5 . The films are sliced into three small pieces (3 × 3 cm in size) in order to accurately measure their thickness. Readings are made using a thickness tester and the average values of the three readings for each film are shown in Table 5. According to the study, a pristine PVA film has a thickness of 0.035 mm, while a PVA@CQDs (700 mg/L) film has a thickness of 0.081 mm and has a small amount of CQDs added as a UV protective agent. In addition, we noticed that as CQDs are added to the PVA matrix, the film thickness increased, ranging from 0.035 mm to 0.081 mm. The findings of show that composite films are thin, flexible and highly elastic and that the thickness of PVA@CQDs (700 mg/L) composite film is not significantly higher than the pristine PVA film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties study of PVA and PVA@CQDs composite films
ce:section.ce:para: It is important to investigate the mechanical properties of polymeric films because of the wide range of uses for them in coating, packing and other sectors. Stress-strain analysis of PVA and PVA@CQDs composite films is done at greater concentrations of CQDs in order to understand the impact of the UV blocking agent (CQDs) introduced on the original mechanical properties of PVA films. The films are tested for their mechanical characteristics using a Universal Tensile Strength machine after being first cut into dimensions of 3 cm × 15 cm × 0.01 cm. The results are reported in Table 6 . According to these findings, pristine PVA films exhibit higher values for stress-strain characteristics such as load at break, extension, tensile strain and tensile stress than PVA@ CQDs composite films. The addition of colloidal CQDs to the films may be the cause of the slight change in stress-strain characteristics that occurs when CQDs are incorporated into PVA matrix [86,87]. The mechanical properties did not significantly change, indicating that the films with CQD incorporation can be used for a wide range of applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Food packaging application using PVA@CQDs CFs
ce:section.ce:para: An experiment has been conducted for the storage of pomegranate seeds with pristine PVA and PVA@CQDs (100, 500 and 900 mg/L) CFs as a UV blocking material up to 9 days (Fig. 14f). As can be seen from the images, no noticeable changes in colour, shrinkage and shape of the pomegranate seeds exposed up to 9 days. However, in the case of pomegranate seeds wrapped in pristine PVA are considerably brownish in colour and shrunken. Based on the aforementioned findings, can conclude that PVA@CQDs CFs are among the next-generation materials and may be effectively employed in food packaging.

Due to its high-stability FL and mechanical qualities, the PVA@CQDs film has a great potential to assemble diverse devices for a variety of possible applications. The proposal of a wearable pH-detector is creatively proposed and created for the benefit of human health. The lactic acid released by working muscles causes human perspiration to have a precise pH distribution between 4 and 8 [88]. As a result, the intelligent detection of pH variation is crucial for reflecting the physiological data of human health [89,90]. Fig. 15 a shows a schematic representation of the wearable pH-detector as a proof-of-concept. The majority of its components are the pH-responsive film, pH-detection module and UV light emitter and collector. This PVA@CQDs film's pH responsiveness is initially assessed prior to application. Upon various pH stimulations, the FL spectra of the film are found (Fig. 15b). The luminous intensity dropped as the pH value rose as is predicted, demonstrating the intended pH-responsiveness in indicating the range of human perspiration. It is important to note that even though the PVA matrix contained the CQDs, the hydroxyl-enriched PVA chains are able to stabilise large amounts of water molecules. As a result, a composite film with exceptionally sensitive responsiveness to pH stimulation is produced, which helped to establish an artificial ion-migration channel for the fast transit of various components in human sweat. Another important factor for this pH-responsive film is FL stability. As a result, the intensity is assessed once again during pH stimulation regulation. Fig. 15c demonstrates that their intensities are continually kept in a stable zone as the pH value remained constant, suggesting an accurate and dependable detection of this film for pH stimulation. This outstanding performance gave the pH-detector we developed a solid foundation. The direct assessment of pH variation as expressed by FL intensity is then carried out in a real-world running processing. Fig. 15d demonstrates that the tightly skin-fitted PVA@CQDs CFs is successful in detecting pH change in human sweat in real-time. Additionally, as the running duration grew, the relative intensity of FL increased, indicating that a drop in pH value accompanied the secretion of sweat. The relative intensity ratio increased to 1.36 after 40 min of operation, indicating that this ongoing processing is subject to significant variance. This wearable pH-detector has a great deal of promise to precisely monitor and detect pH fluctuation in human perspiration while processing movement, in our opinion. As a result, we believe that this CFs high performance and pH responsiveness are likely to be utilized in wearable intelligent devices, enhancing services for human health.

In conclusion, we fabricated a straightforward, effective and high production yield synthetic approach to convert WEPS into luminous CQDs. The chemical functional groups (acid, keto, hydroxyl and amine groups) that are present on the CQD surfaces are visible in the as-produced, 5 nm-sized CQD. In particular, at the 321 nm excitation wavelength for CQD, they demonstrated a maximum QY of 28.65 % with strong photo-stability and an intriguing pH dependant behaviour. Due to the amplification and quenching in the intensity of CQDs emission, it is found that the created system is selective towards cadmium (turn-on) and copper (turn-off) ions. After 11 days of exposure to various CQD concentrations, the root length, germination rate and vigour index of the sprouted seeds have been compared. For optimized CQDs (700 mg/L), the highest value of the various parameters is observed. It is noteworthy that film FL intensity sensitive to pH stimulation. An intelligent pH-detector based on this pH-responsive CFs is technologically developed for real-time sensing and detection of pH changes in human sweat during motion process in order to enhance services that benefits to human health. Additionally, the PVA@CQDs(700 mg/L) films viability for use in packing fruits is examined and the results demonstrated that it has enormous potential as a prepared material for use in the agriculture sector for applications involving the safe packaging of food. Overall, our work offers up a wide range possibilities when it comes to the precise sensing and detecting of wearable intelligent devices, anti-counterfeiting, food packaging and agronomic field in addition to developing an environmentally benign method for the manufacture of high-stability and intense FL materials.

D.H. Sandeep: Formal analysis, Data curation. B.R. Radha Krushna: Formal analysis. N. Navya: Methodology. D.B. Santhosh: Software. S.C. Sharma: Supervision. C. Krithika: Data curation. C. Sridhar: Methodology. V. Nirmal Coumare: Formal analysis. H. Nagabhushana: Writing – original draft, Supervision.
